digest.intro: War, venom, dead stars, and the machine that ate your students' brains.

items.title: NATO Ankara: Ukraine Gets Patriot Manufacturing Rights; Greenland Pressure Resurfaces
items.blurb: Two genuinely consequential moves came out of the Ankara summit in one afternoon: Ukraine received licensing rights to manufacture Patriot air defense systems — a significant shift from aid-dependency toward sovereign production capacity — and Trump publicly leaned on Denmark over Greenland again, turning what seemed like a 2025 provocation into something that looks more like a sustained negotiating posture. The alliance is being reshaped by both moves simultaneously.

items.title: Die Casting Comes Home
items.blurb: A desktop CNC-milled graphite die, a hydraulic cylinder, and a pair of old trampoline springs: that's the entire injection system this builder used to produce die-cast aluminum parts at home. The same graphite serves as both the mold and the injector piston — he tested the process with molten wax first, then moved to aluminum. The detail and dimensional consistency of the output is industrial-grade, and the whole thing makes pressurized-molten-metal casting feel almost casual.

items.title: John Deere's Right-to-Repair Settlement — and Why It Might Not Break Anything
items.blurb: The FTC and five state AGs just secured a settlement requiring Deere to open its diagnostic tools to farmers and independent shops — a genuine policy landmark after years of software-locked tractors. But the more pointed read, circulating in the HN thread via Fight to Repair, argues the settlement is structured in ways that preserve the platform monopoly even while appearing to open it: Deere controls what gets released, on what timeline, and at what price. Worth tracking which version proves right.

items.title: Brown University: In-Person Final, Scores Dropped 50%
items.blurb: Economics professor Roberto Serrano switched to take-home exams after a campus shooting shook him — enrollment in his notoriously hard course immediately spiked. When he required an in-person final, scores fell by half. This isn't an AI story in the narrow sense; it's a story about what happens when a shortcut becomes load-bearing infrastructure for an entire cohort's academic performance, and what 'knowing something' even means anymore in a high-stakes environment full of people who are smart enough to have avoided having to learn things.

items.title: Phlegm: Copper Engraving, Dürer, and Murals Built Like Riddles
items.blurb: Phlegm spent the last two years in near-monastic study of Albrecht Dürer's 'Knight, Death, and the Devil' — collecting old prints, examining them under a microscope, reverse-engineering the line technique — before engraving his own version where every element is replaced with his own iconography while preserving Dürer's exact compositional logic and mark-making approach. He describes his work as floating in a 'grey area' where meaning can't fully cross the barrier between people, which is precisely what makes the images function as mythology rather than illustration.

items.title: How Light 'Knows' the Shortest Path
items.blurb: Fermat's principle says light always takes the path of least time — but how does it 'know' in advance which path that is? Veritasium uses this as a door into Feynman's path integral formulation: light doesn't pick one path, it takes all of them simultaneously, and the non-extremal paths cancel each other out through interference. The question sounds like philosophy and lands squarely in the foundations of quantum mechanics.

items.title: Hunting a Novel Psychedelic in the Mushroom Behind Lilliputian Hallucinations
items.blurb: Mushrooms from the genus Lanmaoa are the only known biological trigger for Lilliputian hallucinations — the experience of watching hordes of tiny, vivid, friendly miniature people moving around your actual furniture. Researchers at the University of Utah just built the first family tree for the genus and found it contains none of the genes for psilocybin or ibotenic acid, the two known psychedelic compounds in fungi. Whatever is causing these visions is chemically novel, and hasn't been identified yet — meaning there's an entirely unknown psychedelic out there, encoded in a mushroom, doing something to the brain that nothing else does.

facts.fact: A builder die-cast aluminum parts at home using a mold carved from graphite and a spring-loaded injector made from a modified hydraulic cylinder and old trampoline parts.

Participants who took the highest dose of retatrutide — a drug targeting three hormone receptors simultaneously — lost over 30% of their body weight over roughly two years, a figure experts are comparing to bariatric surgery outcomes.

A French team monitoring a mid-ocean rift between the Australian and Antarctic plates found that most of the tectonic spreading occurred in a short burst — and some major events happened with no seismic signal at all.

Lilliputian hallucinations — visions of friendly miniature people climbing your furniture — can be triggered by a specific mushroom genus (Lanmaoa) that contains none of the genes for any known psychedelic compound.

When Brown University economics professor Roberto Serrano required an in-person final exam for a course that had seen an unusual enrollment surge, scores dropped by 50% compared to the take-home midterm.

make_something.prompt: Build a small WebGL sketch where light rays pathfind across a scene — not by tracing the correct path, but by visibly simulating many paths simultaneously and letting the 'wrong' ones fade out through destructive interference. The Veritasium piece frames this as the actual physics: light takes all paths and cancels itself except at the extremum. Make the cancellation visible. Even a 2D grid of wave amplitudes summing and colliding would do it — the goal is to see Fermat's principle emerge from chaos rather than be stated.

art_picks.title: Sans Soleil
art_picks.creator: Chris Marker
art_picks.why: The film that invented the essay-film as a form: a meditation on memory, ritual, and how different cultures construct meaning from images — Tokyo, Guinea-Bissau, Iceland — threaded by a single invisible narrator. Every frame is a systems-thinker asking what images actually do to the humans who make and receive them. If you haven't seen it, stop everything.

art_picks.title: Haegue Yang — Selected Works
art_picks.creator: Haegue Yang
art_picks.why: Yang builds sculptures from Venetian blinds, bells, light, and scent that function simultaneously as kinetic objects, cultural symbols, and social rituals — the physical system IS the meaning. Her work is a rare case where formal obsession and anthropological depth are completely inseparable.

art_picks.title: Dragging a Dead Deer Up a Hill
art_picks.creator: Grouper
art_picks.why: Liz Harris went unreasonably deep into a narrow zone — tape hiss, reverb, and folk melody dissolved until they become something closer to architecture than song. It's one of those records that changes the room. The system underneath is grief and texture; the surface is almost unbearably beautiful.

events.name: Yves Saint Laurent and Photography
events.venue: International Center of Photography, 84 Ludlow St, Manhattan
events.why: Nearly 300 photographs and archival objects tracing how YSL used photography not just for promotion but as a system for constructing identity, myth, and cultural authority — with work by Avedon, Newton, Penn, Warhol, and Guy Bourdin alongside contact sheets and campaign materials that show the machinery behind the legend.

events.name: Pierre Huyghe: UUmwelt
events.venue: MoMA Abby Aldrich Rockefeller Sculpture Garden, 11 W 53rd St, Manhattan
events.why: Huyghe placed freestanding screens in MoMA's Sculpture Garden running a machine-learning system trained on fMRI scans of a person imagining images — the work activates in real time from visitor gazes and cancer-cell mutation data, making the boundary between human and machine cognition the actual medium; it's as close to a living artwork as a museum has shown.

events.name: Gabriel Orozco: Public Nature
events.venue: JCDecaux Bus Shelters across New York City (Public Art Fund commission)
events.why: Orozco's first commissioned photographic series — 10 new site-specific images of urban life distributed across the city's bus shelter network — turns the infrastructure of waiting into a gallery and asks what it means to really see the fabric of a city you move through every day.

events.name: SVA MFA Art Practice: "In Plain Sight"
events.venue: SVA Flatiron Gallery, 133 W 21st St, Manhattan
events.why: Opening-night show from SVA's MFA Art Practice graduates, curated by faculty member Jacquelyn Strycker — small graduate shows are where you find artists at their most unguarded and formally adventurous, before the work gets institutionalized; the reception is tonight (July 9, 6–8pm).

events.name: Lincoln Center Summer for the City: Ruidosa Fest
events.venue: Lincoln Center, Josie Robertson Plaza, Upper West Side
events.why: A free outdoor festival focused on Latinx women and non-binary artists in music — part of Lincoln Center's broader Summer for the City series that runs nightly free and pay-what-you-wish events all summer, making the campus itself a kind of rotating public commons.
